So I started a career early on at IBM, and I think one of the first things I did, I was at this IBM security division, and what I realized is, at time I think IBM was maybe spending a billion a year in marketing, and we would just struggle to get attention from the market on any category that we were marketing, right?
And I'm like, wait, hold on.
You could literally take 500 million, and you could buy every publication in every category that you play in.

It's cheaper than marketing, it just takes a little longer, but it's compounding.
And so I kind of always did that my whole career, and that's what kind of set me apart in how I built my, I think, somewhat successful career.
It's like, helping brands be more like publishers, it just changes the mentality.

So it's always the same playbook, but IBM, I built a site called securedintelligence.com, and the whole idea was, okay, I'm going to go hire a managing editor, I'm going to start publishing really high quality content, then I'm going to take that audience and turn it into subscribers, and then I'm going to nurture it, and then eventually like that drives to revenue.
And first year of securedintelligence.com, we invested about 300,000 in content, and we got 17 million in close one revenue, which was like the highest ROI mark.

Marketing program in all of IBM.com, and so much so that the site lasted 15 years, they just folded it back into IBM.com, and as you all probably know, nothing lasts 15 years in almost any company, and so it was a good testament to being like, oh, it kind of worked.
And so then I was poached at HP to build something similar, so then what I did instead was, how do I set this up in a way that the more I do, the cheaper, better, and faster it gets?

And so what I did, I built a contributor network, so I launched this site called techbeacon.com, and I was going after every conference, like RSA, Black Hat, all these conferences that had experts, and I would talk to the speakers and offer them, you know, a dollar a word to publish content under us first, before they published on their own blogs.

And they all loved it, they got to work with an award-winning editor that I poached, and we started publishing 20 articles a week, and we got to about a million visitors a month quickly, and 300,000 subscribers, and then it became the biggest source of leads for the whole company.
